titleStmt.title: Letter from William Upton Tyrrell to Elizabeth Tyrrell 10, October, 1917
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: William Upton Tyrrell

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: Letter from William Upton Tyrrell to his mother.

msIdentifier.repository: OPW-Maynooth University Archive & Research Centre at Castletown

correspAction.placeName: Ballindoolin House, Ballindoolin, Edenderry, Kings Co., Ireland

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
10 October 1917 My dear Mamy Here we are still but having a
very easy time. Nothing to do but eat
and sleep & you may be quite sure
we do plenty of both. I was delighted
to get your long letter last night and
I am sure the apples will come tonight so
I will have a real good feed. I hear there was a great show at
Ypres again yesterday but no details
have come in yet. I am very glad Miss Joly is
going down to see you. She will be
company for you. Next time to you see Mr Bodel ask
him if he remembers a fellow named
R.A Young whom he married some time
ago. He is Capt in this battalion &
is an awfully nice fellow. He comes
from somewhere near Belfast and
is home on leave at present. There was quite a frost here two
nights ago but the general rule
is rain & the trenches are very wet. I cannot write a long letter
as there is no place to get news
except from Johnny over the way
and he is not communicative as
a rule. With very best love to all from Your most affec Son
Willie
